metadata.title: Research What Makes AI Feedback Effective: Evidence Review

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The zoom-level point landed for me: adequate for surface revision, unproven for argumentation. I want to push the evidence review harder. Please (1) rebuild the domain comparison table for writing, math, and programming with sample sizes and one limitation each; (2) name which cited studies have corporate sponsorship and how that shifts my trust in their applicability rating; (3) specify the exact study design that would move 'adequate in narrow contexts' to 'robustly equivalent across domains,' including what outcome it would have to measure.

props.command: The zoom-level point landed for me: adequate for surface revision, unproven for argumentation. I want to push the evidence review harder. Please (1) rebuild the domain comparison table for writing, math, and programming with sample sizes and one limitation each; (2) name which cited studies have corporate sponsorship and how that shifts my trust in their applicability rating; (3) specify the exact study design that would move 'adequate in narrow contexts' to 'robustly equivalent across domains,' including what outcome it would have to measure.
props.segment: Research What Makes AI Feedback Effective:

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Brutalist and Educational AI. Build it with Claude — then take it apart.

Educators are being told AI feedback is as good as human feedback — but the evidence is mixed, domain-dependent, and rarely presented in a form teachers can act on. The claim is true at one zoom level. At the zoom level that matters for your classroom, it may not be.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: problem hook. 'Studies show AI feedback works' — and — 'Studies show AI feedback works for short-answer math at the 5th grade level.' Same sentence, different zoom.

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to find and summarize the best available evidence on AI-generated feedback effectiveness versus human feedback. For each study cited: state the domain, sample size, key finding, and one limitation. Cross-check any study that appears to have corporate sponsorship.

props.command: claude "Find and summarize the best available evidence on AI-generated feedback effectiveness vs. human feedback in K-12 and higher education. For each study you cite: state the domain, sample size, key finding, and one limitation. Cross-check any study that appears to have corporate sponsorship."

beats.narration_text: The research terminal runs the multi-turn prompting sequence: gather, then cross-check, then synthesize, then format. Each step is a separate command. The final output is a comparison table — domain, study design, sample size, key finding, applicability rating.

props.code:
import subprocess

def evidence_review():
    # Step 1: gather
    gather = subprocess.run(
        ["claude",
         "Find 6 studies on AI feedback vs human feedback.\n"
         "For each: domain, N, finding, limitation, sponsorship."],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout
    # Step 2: cross-check
    check = subprocess.run(
        ["claude",
         "Cross-check these citations. Flag any you cannot\n"
         "verify or that have corporate funding conflicts."],
        input=gather, capture_output=True, text=True
    ).stdout
    # Step 3: synthesize
    synth = subprocess.run(
        ["claude",
         "Synthesize into a comparison table:\n"
         "Domain | N | Finding | Limitation | Verified"],
        input=check, capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    print(synth.stdout)

beats.narration_text: The comparison table appears. Three domains: writing, mathematics, programming. Writing: AI feedback adequate for surface-level revision in two controlled studies, but no evidence for argumentation feedback at the level of expert human readers. Mathematics: strong evidence for short-answer correctness feedback. Programming: mixed — error detection good, design feedback poor. The key finding: the zoom level determines the conclusion.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: comparison table. Three domains as rows. Columns: study design, N, key finding, limitation. Writing and programming rows show mixed results. Math row shows stronger but narrow evidence.

beats.narration_text: The change: ask Claude to identify what the evidence would need to show to change the conclusion — what kind of study would move 'AI feedback is adequate in narrow contexts' to 'AI feedback is robustly equivalent to human feedback across domains'?

props.command: claude "Based on this evidence synthesis, what would a study need to show to change the conclusion from 'AI feedback adequate in narrow contexts' to 'AI feedback robustly equivalent to human feedback across domains'? State the required study design, minimum N, domains, and outcome measures."

beats.narration_text: Claude identifies the evidentiary gap: no study has tested AI feedback against expert human feedback on open-ended argumentation tasks with follow-up learning measures beyond one week. The required study: minimum three hundred students, at least two domains, pre-test and post-test at four weeks, blind rating by expert humans and AI on the same submissions. Until that study exists, 'AI feedback works' is true only at the zoom level the existing studies can support.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: evidentiary gap statement. What is missing from current research. Required study design listed as bullet points. The zoom level distinction made explicit.

beats.narration_text: The research terminal reveals the structure of the claim — not whether AI feedback works, but where and when and for what. That structure is what teachers can act on. The zoom level is everything for instructional decisions.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: teardown summary. 'Same sentence, different zoom levels.' The evidentiary structure as the actionable finding.

beats.narration_text: Next: Build a Lesson AI-Integration Audit — classify the AI's role in each lesson activity as central, peripheral, or decorative.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research What Makes AI Feedback Effective: Evidence Review] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
